title: Wet-felted headband
subtitle: A simple padded merino headband, fulled to a firm, flexible band
excerpt: Wet-felt a merino headband by working roving around a foam resist cut to headband shape. The finished piece is about 5 cm wide, comfortably padded, and holds its shape on the head without wire or interfacing.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from traditional wet-felting practice. Headbands felted over a resist are a well-established beginner project across British felt-making circles.

glossaryTerms.definition: A felting method using hot soapy water and agitation to lock wool fibres together.
The stage of wet felting where more vigorous manipulation shrinks the fabric to the desired size and tightens the fibre structure.

content.text: A felted headband is made by wet-felting around a resist cut to headband shape from thick foam rubber or craft foam.
produces a naturally padded band that holds its curve without stiffening.
shrinks the band to the correct head circumference. Fine merino gives a soft, resilient surface comfortable enough for extended wear.

items.name: Foam rubber or craft foam resist template

content.text: Cut a headband resist from thick foam rubber: a curved arc about 38 cm along the outer edge, 5 cm wide, and shaped to sit from ear to ear across the top of the head. The resist should be about 25% larger than the desired finished headband to allow for shrinkage.
Step 2: Lay and wet the roving

Tease the roving into thin wisps and lay a layer over the resist on both sides, keeping the thickness even and the edges extending about 1.5 cm past the resist edge. Wet thoroughly with hot soapy water and press flat. Add a second perpendicular layer.

Rub with a soapy flat palm for 8 minutes per side, turning frequently. The roving will consolidate and begin to grip the resist. Roll the band under your palm on the mat, then rinse in cool water. Slide the resist out. Full the band by rolling and rubbing without the resist until the desired firmness is reached.
While still damp, curve the band over a head-sized ball or bowl to dry in its correct arc. Allow to dry fully before wearing.

items.problem: The headband is too large and slips on the head
items.fix: Wet the band in hot water and full it further by rolling and squeezing. Check the size regularly against a head circumference measurement.

items.problem: The surface is fluffy rather than smooth
items.cause: The rubbing phase was too short to lock down the surface fibres
items.fix: Rub the surface with a soapy palm using firm, flat strokes until the surface fibres lock down. A final rinse in cool water and gentle pressing with a towel gives the cleanest result.
